name: Repeated password abuse, leak password recovery
description: How to respond to a situation where a leaked password from one service puts other accounts at risk.
label: Password

scenario.title: Leaked password from one account
scenario.setting: The message tells you that one of the online services you were using has been hacked. Soon after, you will notice attempts to log into your e-mail and other services with the same password.
scenario.objective: User must prevent chain takeover of accounts.

milestones.name: Instant change of passwords on all accounts

scenario.tutorial: Using the same password on multiple services allows chain attacks. After a password leak, all related passwords must be changed and 2FA activated.

content.text: Credential Stuffing: A brute force attack

When a website's password database is leaked, attackers immediately upload those email and password combinations to automated bots. They then test this data on hundreds of other popular services – from Facebook and Netflix to internet banking. If you use the same password in multiple places, it's only a matter of time before a bot hits. This process is called Credential Stuffing and is responsible for most successful account takeovers.

Your email is the most important account you have. If an attacker gains access to it, they can use the 'Forgot Password' feature to have recovery links sent to all other services you have associated with this email. This gives them full control over your digital identity, even if you have unique passwords on other sites. Securing your email with the strongest possible password and two-factor authentication is an absolute priority.

The only secure way to manage your digital identity is to use a unique and randomly generated password for each service. Since the human brain cannot remember hundreds of complex combinations, it is necessary to use a password manager. It remembers all passwords for you and fills them in automatically. You only need to remember one single, very strong 'master password' for this administrator.
How to know if your password has been leaked?

There are services like 'Have I Been Pwned' which collect information about compromised databases. Just enter your email to find out if and where your data has been compromised. Modern browsers and password managers will also automatically alert you if they detect that your password has appeared in a known data breach. If you receive such a warning, take it seriously and change your password immediately.

If you find that your password has been leaked, follow these steps: 
1. Immediately change the password on the compromised service.
2. Identify all other accounts where you have used this password and change them as well (each to a different one!).
3. Enable two-factor authentication (2FA) wherever possible. 
4. Check the login history in the account settings and log out all unknown devices. 
5. Check whether the attacker has set automatic forwarding of messages to his address in the e-mail.
